summary: Warm, well‑mixed soundboard capture of the historic Wall‑of‑Sound debut, highlighted by a smoking Mexicali, epic Tennessee solo, blazing El Paso and a standout UJB‑Playin sandwich.
review: The recording is generally warm and spacious – most listeners praise the soundboard (Dick’s #24) and the LAI.1186 sources as clear, with the Wall‑of‑Sound miking giving an antiseptic separation that adds character. Musically the show is uneven; early songs stumble but later moments – Mexicali, Jerry’s Tennessee solo, El Paso, the UJB‑Playin jam and several ’74 classics – shine, making it a historically interesting if not top‑tier performance.

ai_rating.rationale: Two reviewers rave about the warm, well‑balanced recording and strong late‑set performances; one reviewer finds the overall show average with mixed sound quality. The blend of high musical moments and decent technical fidelity yields a rating just above four stars.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Soundboard (Dick’s #24) is clear with the Wall‑of‑Sound's characteristic antiseptic separation; LAI.1186 source adds warmth and spaciousness. Some reviewers note occasional tuning glitches that affect performance, not the mix itself.

show_quality.setlist_flow: The set is unusually long with no Drums>Space, giving a song‑heavy first set that warms up slowly. After a rough start the second half builds momentum, peaking with the UJB‑Playin sequence and several high‑energy jams before losing steam toward the encore.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Early in the show his guitar is detuned, but his solo on Tennessee is praised as ‘careening’ and he delivers a powerful El Paso later; overall reviewers consider him extremely well‑playing.
band_member_comments.Phil: Generally solid throughout; one reviewer calls him “on fire,” while another notes he’s more reserved compared with earlier ’74 shows.
band_member_comments.Bob: Bobby’s contributions (Deal, Me & My Uncle) are highlighted as strong; a reviewer says his vocals tease the newbies and he’s “on fire” on the AUD versions.
band_member_comments.Keys: Keith is praised in one review for being “on fire,” though his work on the debut of ‘Cassidy’ is described as a train‑wreck that led the band to shelve it until ’76.
